summary: Patrick McKenzie defends his choice to use PayPal for his software business despite widespread community fears about account freezes. He argues that alternative payment processors like eSellerate, Avantage, and Plimus cost significantly more — hundreds of dollars extra on modest profits — while offering worse customer experiences. He frames PayPal as a calculated risk comparable to depending on any other business infrastructure, and notes that 70% of his customers already have PayPal accounts, making it a trust signal. He concludes with practical risk mitigation strategies like auto-sweeping balances to a bank account.
key_points: Alternative payment processors would have cost $150-$270 extra on ~$1,250 in pre-tax profit, a massive percentage hit that scales linearly with sales
Customer trust for PayPal far exceeds trust for an unknown indie developer — 70% of his customers already have PayPal accounts
Every business relationship involves calculated risk; PayPal freezes are no different from Google de-indexing you or your bank losing records
PayPal's ecosystem spawned useful services like e-junkie and Payloadz that further reduce costs and friction
Risk can be mitigated practically by auto-sweeping balances daily to a bank account, capping maximum exposure to ~$50
money_quotes: Trust is currency, because without trust I get no currency.
When phenomenally successful Andy Brice asks 'What am I getting for an extra $880 per thousand transactions?' that is not a hypothetical question.
My relationship with Paypal, like my relationship with my bank, with Google, and with the Java community, is a calculated risk.

Whereas I have never lost a dime on Paypal in 7 years, I have never successfully recouped a penny off of close to $100 in sales on eSellerate due to obtusely difficult processes to connect me to my money.
The Paypal name means 'Buy here and you're covered' to the astoundingly high seventy percent of my customers who have an account with them.

This limits my maximum losses on Paypal to approximately $50, plus whatever damage to my business continuity the freeze would cause before I could type 'mv purchasing_esellerate.htm purchasing.htm' into a terminal somewhere.
key_insight: For a small software business, the concrete costs of alternative payment processors and the trust value of a recognized brand like PayPal far outweigh the speculative risk of an account freeze, especially when that risk can be mitigated to near-zero with simple balance sweeping.
